title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Installing and Launching R Packages

pacman::p_load(corrplot, ggstatsplot, tidyverse)

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
section: Importing and Preparing The Data Set
text:
Importing and Preparing The Data Set
Wine Quality Data Set of UCI Machine Learning Repository will be used. The data set consists of 13 variables and 6497 observations. For the purpose of this exercise, we have combined the red wine and white wine data into one data file. It is called wine_quality and is in csv file format.

wine <- read_csv("data/wine_quality.csv")

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Building Correlation Matrix: pairs() method
Syntax description of pairs function.

Building a basic correlation matrix
Figure below shows the scatter plot matrix of Wine Quality Data. It is a 11 by 11 matrix.

pairs(wine[,1:11])








The required input of pairs() can be a matrix or data frame. The code chunk used to create the scatterplot matrix is relatively simple. It uses the default pairs function. Columns 2 to 12 of wine dataframe is used to build the scatterplot matrix. The variables are: fixed acidity, volatile acidity, citric acid, residual sugar, chlorides, free sulfur dioxide, total sulfur dioxide, density, pH, sulphates and alcohol.

pairs(wine[,2:12])










Drawing the lower/upper corner
pairs function of R Graphics provided many customisation arguments. For example, it is a common practice to show either the upper half or lower half of the correlation matrix instead of both. This is because a correlation matrix is symmetric.
To show the lower half of the correlation matrix, the upper.panel argument will be used as shown in the code chunk below.
pairs(wine[,2:12], lower.panel = NULL)
pairs(wine[,2:12], upper.panel = NULL)












Including with correlation coefficients
To show the correlation coefficient of each pair of variables instead of a scatter plot, panel.cor function will be used. This will also show higher correlations in a larger font.

panel.cor <- function(x, y, digits=2, prefix="", cex.cor, ...) {
  usr <- par("usr")
  on.exit(par(usr))
  par(usr = c(0, 1, 0, 1))
  r <- abs(cor(x, y, use="complete.obs"))
  txt <- format(c(r, 0.123456789), digits=digits)[1]
  txt <- paste(prefix, txt, sep="")
  if(missing(cex.cor)) cex.cor <- 0.8/strwidth(txt)
  text(0.5, 0.5, txt, cex = cex.cor * (1 + r) / 2)
}

pairs(wine[,2:12],
      upper.panel = panel.cor)

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Visualising Correlation Matrix: ggcormat()
One of the major limitation of the correlation matrix is that the scatter plots appear very cluttered when the number of observations is relatively large (i.e. more than 500 observations). To overcome this problem, Corrgram data visualisation technique suggested by D. J. Murdoch and E. D. Chow (1996) and Friendly, M (2002) and will be used.
The are at least three R packages provide function to plot corrgram, they are:

corrgram
ellipse
corrplot

On top that, some R package like ggstatsplot package also provides functions for building corrgram.
In this section, you will learn how to visualising correlation matrix by using ggcorrmat() of ggstatsplot package.

The basic plot
One of the advantage of using ggcorrmat() over many other methods to visualise a correlation matrix is it’s ability to provide a comprehensive and yet professional statistical report as shown in the figure below.

ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat(
  data = wine,
  cor.vars = 1:11)









ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat(
  data = wine,
  cor.vars = 1:11,
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black",
                         hc.order = TRUE,
                         tl.cex = 10),
  title    = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
  subtitle = "Four pairs are no significant at p < 0.05"
)








Things to learn from the code chunk above:

cor.vars argument is used to compute the correlation matrix needed to build the corrgram.
ggcorrplot.args argument provide additional (mostly aesthetic) arguments that will be passed to ggcorrplot::ggcorrplot function. The list should avoid any of the following arguments since they are already internally being used: corr, method, p.mat, sig.level, ggtheme, colors, lab, pch, legend.title, digits.

The sample sub-code chunk can be used to control specific component of the plot such as the font size of the x-axis, y-axis, and the statistical report.

ggplot.component = list(
    theme(text=element_text(size=5),
      axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8),
      axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8)))

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Building multiple plots
Since ggstatsplot is an extension of ggplot2, it also supports faceting. However the feature is not available in ggcorrmat() but in the grouped_ggcorrmat() of ggstatsplot.

grouped_ggcorrmat(
  data = wine,
  cor.vars = 1:11,
  grouping.var = type,
  type = "robust",
  p.adjust.method = "holm",
  plotgrid.args = list(ncol = 2),
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black",
                         hc.order = TRUE,
                         tl.cex = 10),
  annotation.args = list(
    tag_levels = "a",
    title = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
    subtitle = "The measures are: alcohol, sulphates, fixed acidity, citric acid, chlorides, residual sugar, density, free sulfur dioxide and volatile acidity",
    caption = "Dataset: UCI Machine Learning Repository"
  )
)








Things to learn from the code chunk above:

to build a facet plot, the only argument needed is grouping.var.
Behind group_ggcorrmat(), patchwork package is used to create the multiplot. plotgrid.args argument provides a list of additional arguments passed to patchwork::wrap_plots, except for guides argument which is already separately specified earlier.
Likewise, annotation.args argument is calling plot annotation arguments of patchwork package.

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
section: Visualising Correlation Matrix using corrplot Package
text:
Visualising Correlation Matrix using corrplot Package
In this hands-on exercise, we will focus on corrplot. However, you are encouraged to explore the other two packages too.
Before getting started, you are required to read An Introduction to corrplot Package in order to gain basic understanding of corrplot package.

Getting started with corrplot
Before we can plot a corrgram using corrplot(), we need to compute the correlation matrix of wine data frame.
In the code chunk below, cor() of R Stats is used to compute the correlation matrix of wine data frame.

wine.cor <- cor(wine[, 1:11])

Next, corrplot() is used to plot the corrgram by using all the default setting as shown in the code chunk below.

corrplot(wine.cor)








Notice that the default visual object used to plot the corrgram is circle. The default layout of the corrgram is a symmetric matrix. The default colour scheme is diverging blue-red. Blue colours are used to represent pair variables with positive correlation coefficients and red colours are used to represent pair variables with negative correlation coefficients. The intensity of the colour or also known as saturation is used to represent the strength of the correlation coefficient. Darker colours indicate relatively stronger linear relationship between the paired variables. On the other hand, lighter colours indicates relatively weaker linear relationship.


Working with visual geometrics
In corrplot package, there are seven visual geometrics (parameter method) can be used to encode the attribute values. They are: circle, square, ellipse, number, shade, color and pie. The default is circle. As shown in the previous section, the default visual geometric of corrplot matrix is circle. However, this default setting can be changed by using the method argument as shown in the code chunk below.

corrplot(wine.cor,
         method = "ellipse")








Feel free to change the method argument to other supported visual geometrics.


Working with layout
corrplot() supports three layout types, namely: “full”, “upper” or “lower”. The default is “full” which display full matrix. The default setting can be changed by using the type argument of corrplot().

corrplot(wine.cor,
         method = "ellipse",
         type="lower")








The default layout of the corrgram can be further customised. For example, arguments diag and tl.col are used to turn off the diagonal cells and to change the axis text label colour to black colour respectively as shown in the code chunk and figure below.

corrplot(wine.cor,
         method = "ellipse",
         type="lower",
         diag = FALSE,
         tl.col = "black")








Please feel free to experiment with other layout design argument such as tl.pos, tl.cex, tl.offset, cl.pos, cl.cex and cl.offset, just to mention a few of them.


Working with mixed layout
With corrplot package, it is possible to design corrgram with mixed visual matrix of one half and numerical matrix on the other half. In order to create a corrgram with mixed layout, the corrplot.mixed(), a wrapped function for mixed visualisation style will be used.
Figure below shows a mixed layout corrgram plotted using wine quality data.

corrplot.mixed(wine.cor,
               lower = "ellipse",
               upper = "number",
               tl.pos = "lt",
               diag = "l",
               tl.col = "black")








Notice that argument lower and upper are used to define the visualisation method used. In this case ellipse is used to map the lower half of the corrgram and numerical matrix (i.e. number) is used to map the upper half of the corrgram. The argument tl.pos, on the other, is used to specify the placement of the axis label. Lastly, the diag argument is used to specify the glyph on the principal diagonal of the corrgram.


Combining corrgram with the significant test
In statistical analysis, we are also interested to know which pair of variables their correlation coefficients are statistically significant.
Figure below shows a corrgram combined with the significant test. The corrgram reveals that not all correlation pairs are statistically significant. For example the correlation between total sulfur dioxide and free sulfur dioxide is statistically significant at significant level of 0.1 but not the pair between total sulfur dioxide and citric acid.
With corrplot package, we can use the cor.mtest() to compute the p-values and confidence interval for each pair of variables.

wine.sig = cor.mtest(wine.cor, conf.level= .95)

We can then use the p.mat argument of corrplot function as shown in the code chunk below.

corrplot(wine.cor,
         method = "number",
         type = "lower",
         diag = FALSE,
         tl.col = "black",
         tl.srt = 45,
         p.mat = wine.sig$p,
         sig.level = .05)










Reorder a corrgram
Matrix reorder is very important for mining the hidden structure and pattern in a corrgram. By default, the order of attributes of a corrgram is sorted according to the correlation matrix (i.e. “original”). The default setting can be over-written by using the order argument of corrplot(). Currently, corrplot package support four sorting methods, they are:

“AOE” is for the angular order of the eigenvectors. See Michael Friendly (2002) for details.
“FPC” for the first principal component order.
“hclust” for hierarchical clustering order, and “hclust.method” for the agglomeration method to be used.

“hclust.method” should be one of “ward”, “single”, “complete”, “average”, “mcquitty”, “median” or “centroid”.

“alphabet” for alphabetical order.

More algorithms can be found in seriation package.

corrplot.mixed(wine.cor,
               lower = "ellipse",
               upper = "number",
               tl.pos = "lt",
               diag = "l",
               order="AOE",
               tl.col = "black")










Reordering a correlation matrix using hclust
If using hclust, corrplot() can draw rectangles around the corrgram based on the results of hierarchical clustering.

corrplot(wine.cor,
         method = "ellipse",
         tl.pos = "lt",
         tl.col = "black",
         order="hclust",
         hclust.method = "ward.D",
         addrect = 3)

text:
Reference
Michael Friendly (2002). “Corrgrams: Exploratory displays for correlation matrices”. The American Statistician, 56, 316–324.
D.J. Murdoch, E.D. Chow (1996). “A graphical display of large correlation matrices”. The American Statistician, 50, 178–180.
Kam T. S. (2023). R for Visual Analytics

R packages

ggcorrmat() of ggstatsplot package
ggscatmat and ggpairs of GGally.
corrplot. A graphical display of a correlation matrix or general matrix. It also contains some algorithms to do matrix reordering. In addition, corrplot is good at details, including choosing color, text labels, color labels, layout, etc.
corrgram calculates correlation of variables and displays the results graphically. Included panel functions can display points, shading, ellipses, and correlation values with confidence intervals.

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Heatmaps encode data values as colour variations across a grid, making them excellent for exploring multivariate datasets. When applied to a tabular format, rows typically represent observations (e.g., countries) and columns represent variables (e.g., happiness indicators).
What heatmaps are good for:

Showing variance across multiple variables at a glance
Revealing clusters and patterns across rows and columns
Detecting correlations and similarities between variables
Comparing many observations simultaneously

This chapter covers both static and interactive heatmaps using R.

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Heatmaps encode data values as colour variations across a grid, making them excellent for exploring multivariate datasets. When applied to a tabular format, rows typically represent observations (e.g., countries) and columns represent variables (e.g., happiness indicators).
What heatmaps are good for:

Showing variance across multiple variables at a glance
Revealing clusters and patterns across rows and columns
Detecting correlations and similarities between variables
Comparing many observations simultaneously

This chapter covers both static and interactive heatmaps using R.

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Installing and Launching R Packages
Four packages are needed for this chapter:

pacman::p_load(seriation, dendextend, heatmaply, tidyverse)




Package
Role




tidyverse
Data import and wrangling


heatmaply
Interactive heatmaps


dendextend
Dendrogram analysis and optimal cluster selection


seriation
Optimal row/column ordering algorithms

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
section: Importing and Preparing the Data Set
text:
Importing and Preparing the Data Set
The dataset used is the World Happiness Report 2018 (WHData-2018.csv).

Importing the data set

wh <- read_csv("data/WHData-2018.csv")

read_csv() from readr imports the file as a tibble data frame stored in wh.


Preparing the data
Row names are set to country names (instead of row numbers) to make the heatmap axis labels meaningful:

row.names(wh) <- wh$Country



Transforming the data frame into a matrix
Heatmap functions require a matrix object, not a data frame. We select the relevant numeric columns and convert:

wh1 <- dplyr::select(wh, c(3, 7:12))   # select numeric columns of interest
wh_matrix <- data.matrix(wh)            # convert to matrix


Key concept: data.matrix() coerces all columns to numeric, producing an R matrix suitable for heatmap functions.

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Static Heatmap
Several R packages can draw static heatmaps:







Function / Package
Description




heatmap() — base R stats
Simple heatmap with optional dendrograms


heatmap.2() — gplots
Enhanced version of base heatmap


pheatmap() — pheatmap
“Pretty Heatmap” with more layout control


ComplexHeatmap — Bioconductor
Complex, annotated heatmaps (genomics-oriented)


superheat
Highly customisable; handles big and multi-type data



This chapter focuses on heatmap() from base R stats.

heatmap() of R Stats
Without clustering (plain heatmap):

wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)









Rowv = NA and Colv = NA suppress row and column dendrograms.
The result is a simple colour grid in the original data order.

With clustering (default behaviour):

wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix)









By default, heatmap() reorders rows and columns using hierarchical clustering (distance-based).
Dendrograms are drawn on the sides to reflect the clustering structure.
The reordering groups similar countries and variables together.

With column normalisation (recommended):

wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      scale  = "column",
                      cexRow = 0.6,
                      cexCol = 0.8,
                      margins = c(10, 4))









Why normalise? Variables like “Happiness Score” have much larger raw values than others (e.g., “Generosity”). Without scaling, the colour variation is dominated by high-range variables, making all others look identical. Column scaling (subtract mean, divide by SD) brings variables onto a comparable scale.

Key arguments:



Argument
Purpose




scale = "column"
Normalise each column (z-score)


cexRow / cexCol
Font size for row / column labels


margins = c(col, row)
Extra space for long axis labels

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Creating Interactive Heatmap
heatmaply builds interactive cluster heatmaps exportable as standalone HTML. It wraps plotly under the hood, enabling zoom, hover tooltips, and pan.

Working with heatmaply
Basic usage:

heatmaply(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)])





Differences from base heatmap():

The horizontal dendrogram appears on the left side (not top).
Row text labels are on the right side.
Long x-axis labels are auto-rotated 135°.




Data Transformation
Because multivariate datasets often mix variables with different units and ranges, data transformation before clustering is essential. heatmaply supports three methods:

Scaling (Z-score)
When to use: Variables are assumed to come from (approximately) normal distributions.
How it works: Subtract the column mean and divide by the standard deviation → values represent distance from the mean in SD units.

heatmaply(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)],
          scale = "column")







Normalising (Min-Max)
When to use: Variables come from different, possibly non-normal distributions.
How it works: Subtract the minimum, divide by the maximum → rescales each variable to [0, 1]. Preserves the shape of each variable’s distribution.

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))







Percentising (Empirical CDF)
When to use: You want each value to have a clear percentile interpretation.
How it works: Replaces each value with its empirical percentile rank. A value of 0.75 means 75% of observations are at or below it.

heatmaply(percentize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))






Summary of transformation choices:



Method
Input assumption
Output range
Interpretation




Scale
Normal distribution
~(-3, 3)
SD from mean


Normalize
Any distribution
[0, 1]
Relative magnitude


Percentize
Any distribution
[0, 1]
Percentile rank








Clustering Algorithm
heatmaply supports hierarchical clustering with configurable distance and linkage methods.
Key arguments:








Argument
Default
Options




distfun
dist (Euclidean)
"pearson", "spearman", "kendall" for correlation-based


dist_method
"euclidean"
"maximum", "manhattan", "canberra", "binary", "minkowski"


hclustfun
hclust
—


hclust_method
"complete"
"ward.D", "ward.D2", "single", "average", "mcquitty", "median", "centroid"



Clustering can be calibrated manually or statistically.



Manual Approach
Specify distance and linkage methods directly:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method  = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "ward.D")





Ward.D minimises total within-cluster variance, tending to produce compact, roughly equal-sized clusters.



Statistical Approach
Use dendextend functions to objectively select the best method and number of clusters.
Step 1 — Find the best clustering method using dend_expend():

wh_d <- dist(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]), method = "euclidean")
dend_expend(wh_d)[[3]]

  dist_methods hclust_methods     optim
1      unknown         ward.D 0.6137851
2      unknown        ward.D2 0.6289186
3      unknown         single 0.4774362
4      unknown       complete 0.6434009
5      unknown        average 0.6701688
6      unknown       mcquitty 0.5020102
7      unknown         median 0.5901833
8      unknown       centroid 0.6338734


This compares multiple linkage methods by their optimisation score. The method with the highest optim value is recommended. For the WHR 2018 data, "average" (UPGMA) gives the best result.
Step 2 — Find the optimal number of clusters using find_k():

wh_clust <- hclust(wh_d, method = "average")
num_k <- find_k(wh_clust)
plot(num_k)








The plot suggests k = 3 as the optimal number of clusters.
Step 3 — Apply the statistical findings:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method   = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "average",
          k_row         = 3)





k_row = 3 highlights the three clusters with coloured row side-bars.



Seriation
Hierarchical clustering determines which items are near each other in the tree, but leaves the branch flip order ambiguous. Seriation finds the optimal leaf ordering to minimise visual jumps between adjacent rows/columns.

Analogy: Think of it as solving a restricted Travelling Salesman Problem — find the path through all leaves that minimises total distance, subject to the dendrogram structure.

heatmaply uses the seriation package and offers four seriate options:








Option
Algorithm
Notes




"OLO"
Optimal Leaf Ordering
Default; exact optimum in O(n⁴)


"GW"
Gruvaeus & Wainer
Faster heuristic; near-optimal


"mean"
Mean-based
Matches default behaviour of gplots::heatmap.2


"none"
No rotation
Uses raw dendrogram without reordering




# Optimal Leaf Ordering (default)
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]), seriate = "OLO")




# Gruvaeus-Wainer heuristic
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]), seriate = "GW")




# Mean-based (gplots-compatible)
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]), seriate = "mean")




# No seriation
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]), seriate = "none")








Working with Colour Palettes
The default palette in heatmaply is viridis (perceptually uniform, colourblind-friendly). Alternative palettes from RColorBrewer can be specified via the colors argument:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          seriate = "none",
          colors  = Blues)





Choose palettes that reflect the data’s nature:

Sequential (e.g., Blues, Greens): for data with a natural order from low to high.
Diverging (e.g., RdBu): for data with a meaningful midpoint (e.g., z-scores).
Qualitative: not appropriate for continuous heatmap cells.




The Finishing Touch
heatmaply provides extensive arguments for publication-quality output:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          Colv         = NA,
          seriate      = "none",
          colors       = Blues,
          k_row        = 5,
          margins      = c(NA, 200, 60, NA),
          fontsize_row = 4,
          fontsize_col = 5,
          main = "World Happiness Score and Variables by Country, 2018\nData Transformation using Normalise Method",
          xlab = "World Happiness Indicators",
          ylab = "World Countries")





Key finishing arguments:







Argument
Purpose




k_row
Number of row cluster groups to highlight


margins
Margins in order: left, right, top, bottom


fontsize_row / fontsize_col
Label font sizes (use small values for many rows)


main
Plot title (\n for line breaks)


xlab / ylab
Axis titles


Colv = NA
Suppress column dendrogram

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Key Takeaways

Always transform your data before clustering in a heatmap. The right method depends on your data’s distribution — use scale for normal, normalize for bounded, percentize for rank-based interpretation.
Use statistical methods (dend_expend() + find_k()) to objectively choose clustering method and number of clusters rather than guessing.
Seriation improves readability — OLO or GW ordering makes patterns clearer than default dendrogram ordering.
heatmaply > base heatmap() for most analytical work: it’s interactive, supports transformations natively, integrates seriation, and allows fine-grained aesthetic control.
Colour palette choice matters — viridis is the safe default; Blues or other sequential palettes work for normalised (0–1) data; diverging palettes suit z-scores.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
A parallel coordinates plot (PCP) is a multivariate visualisation technique where each variable is represented as a vertical axis, and each observation is drawn as a polyline connecting its values across all axes. It is specially designed for comparing multiple numerical variables simultaneously and surfacing relationships between them.
Key characteristics:

Invented by Alfred Inselberg in the 1970s for high-dimensional data visualisation.
Better suited for analytical exploration than for communication to general audiences. As Stephen Few (2006) noted, its strength lies in revealing meaningful multivariate patterns and comparisons when used interactively — not in presenting conclusions to a board or the public.
Practical use case: characterising clusters found during customer segmentation, or comparing indicators like those in the World Happiness Index.

Two approaches towards multivariate analysis using parallel coordinate plots:



Approach
Package
Function




Static PCP
GGally
ggparcoord()


Interactive PCP
parallelPlot
parallelPlot()

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Data Preparation
The same World Happiness Report 2018 dataset from Chapter 14 is used (WHData-2018.csv).

wh <- read_csv("data/WHData-2018.csv")

The tibble wh contains columns for country name, region, happiness score, and six contributing variables (columns 7–12):

GDP per capita
Social support
Healthy life expectancy
Freedom to make life choices
Generosity
Perceptions of corruption

No additional matrix transformation is needed here, ggparcoord() and parallelPlot() work directly with data frames.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Plotting Static Parallel Coordinates Plot
Static PCPs are built using ggparcoord() from the GGally package. Because GGally extends ggplot2, standard ggplot2 functions like facet_wrap() and theme() can be combined with it.

Plotting a Simple Parallel Coordinates

ggparcoord(data    = wh,
           columns = c(7:12))









data: the data frame.
columns: integer vector of column indices to use as parallel axes.


This minimal plot is hard to read — all lines share the same colour, variables are on different scales, and there is no grouping. The following sections progressively improve it.




Plotting a Parallel Coordinates with Boxplot

ggparcoord(data        = wh,
           columns     = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale       = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines  = 0.2,
           boxplot     = TRUE,
           title       = "Parallel Coordinates Plot of World Happiness Variables")








Arguments explained:








Argument
Value
Effect




groupColumn
2 (Region)
Colours each polyline by the observation’s region


scale
"uniminmax"
Scales each variable so its min = 0 and max = 1; enables fair comparison across axes


alphaLines
0.2
Sets line transparency (0 = invisible, 1 = fully opaque); reduces overplotting clutter


boxplot
TRUE
Overlays a boxplot on each axis to show the variable’s distribution


title
string
Adds a plot title




Why uniminmax? Without scaling, axes with larger raw ranges (e.g., GDP) dominate visually. uniminmax brings all variables to the same [0, 1] range without assuming normality (unlike z-score scaling).

Other scale options available in ggparcoord():







Value
Description




"std"
Subtract mean, divide by SD (z-score)


"robust"
Subtract median, divide by MAD


"uniminmax"
Min = 0, Max = 1 per variable


"globalminmax"
Scales using global min/max across all selected variables


"center"
Centers each variable at a chosen scaleSummary statistic


"centerObs"
Centers on a specific observation






Parallel Coordinates with Facet
Since ggparcoord() returns a ggplot object, ggplot2 functions can be appended with +.
facet_wrap() splits the plot into small multiples — one panel per region:

ggparcoord(data        = wh,
           columns     = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale       = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines  = 0.2,
           boxplot     = TRUE,
           title       = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region)









Each of the 10 panels shows the parallel coordinates for one geographic region.
This makes within-region patterns much clearer than a single overcrowded plot.


Aesthetic issue: With many variables, the x-axis labels overlap. The next two sections fix this.




Rotating x-axis Text Label
Use theme() with axis.text.x to rotate labels:

ggparcoord(data        = wh,
           columns     = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale       = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines  = 0.2,
           boxplot     = TRUE,
           title       = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30))









angle = 30: rotates x-axis labels 30° from horizontal.


Problem: Rotating without adjusting position causes labels to overlap with the plot area.




Adjusting the Rotated x-axis Text Label
Fix overlapping by adding hjust = 1 to shift the text anchor to the right end of the label:

ggparcoord(data        = wh,
           columns     = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale       = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines  = 0.2,
           boxplot     = TRUE,
           title       = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))









hjust = 1: right-justifies the text, aligning the label’s right edge with the axis tick, preventing overlap with the plot area.


Rule of thumb: Whenever rotating axis text with angle > 0, always pair it with hjust = 1 (and sometimes vjust = 1) to keep labels properly anchored.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
section: Plotting Interactive Parallel Coordinates Plot: parallelPlot Methods
text:
Plotting Interactive Parallel Coordinates Plot: parallelPlot Methods
The parallelPlot package renders interactive parallel coordinates using htmlwidgets and D3.js. Key interactive features include:

Clicking on an axis recolours all lines by that variable’s values (intensity-based colour encoding).
Brushing on any axis filters the visible lines to observations within the selected range.


Data Preparation for parallelPlot
Before plotting, select only the columns of interest (all must be numeric):

wh <- wh %>%
  select("Happiness score", c(7:12))




The Basic Plot

parallelPlot(wh,
             width  = 320,
             height = 250)






width / height: pixel dimensions of the widget.
Default colour: monotonous blue for all lines.
Issue: Long axis labels may overlap — fixed in the next step.




Rotate Axis Label

parallelPlot(wh,
             rotateTitle = TRUE)






rotateTitle = TRUE: rotates all axis labels 90° to prevent overlap.


Interactive tip: After rotating, clicking on any axis variable (e.g., “Happiness score”) changes the colour scheme from uniform blue to a graduated blue intensity — lines are coloured by that variable’s value, immediately revealing correlations.




Changing the Colour Scheme
Replace the default blue with any named D3/ColorBrewer continuous palette:

parallelPlot(wh,
             continuousCS = "YlOrRd",
             rotateTitle  = TRUE)






continuousCS: sets the continuous colour scale used when an axis is clicked.
"YlOrRd" (Yellow-Orange-Red) is a sequential palette that clearly encodes magnitude — high values appear red, low values appear yellow.




Parallel Coordinates Plot with Histogram
Histograms can be displayed along each axis to show marginal distributions:

histoVisibility <- rep(TRUE, ncol(wh))

parallelPlot(wh,
             rotateTitle     = TRUE,
             histoVisibility = histoVisibility)






histoVisibility: a logical vector with one element per column. TRUE shows a histogram for that axis; FALSE hides it.
rep(TRUE, ncol(wh)) enables histograms for all columns.
This gives an at-a-glance view of each variable’s distribution alongside the parallel lines.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Comparing ggparcoord() vs parallelPlot()








Feature
ggparcoord() (GGally)
parallelPlot() (parallelPlot)




Output type
Static ggplot2 object
Interactive HTML widget


Scaling
Built-in via scale argument
Manual pre-processing required


Grouping / colour
Via groupColumn
Via axis click (interactive)


Boxplots on axes
Yes (boxplot = TRUE)
No


Faceting
Yes (via facet_wrap)
No


Histograms on axes
No
Yes (histoVisibility)


Brushing / filtering
No
Yes (D3-powered)


Best for
Publication figures, faceted comparisons
Exploratory analysis, interactive dashboards

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Key Takeaways

Parallel coordinates are for exploration, not communication. They excel at revealing multivariate patterns when used interactively but are not suitable for general audiences or executive presentations.
Always scale your variables. Raw values on different ranges make PCPs unreadable. uniminmax in ggparcoord() is the most intuitive choice; it preserves the shape of each variable without assuming normality.
Use groupColumn to add colour. Colouring lines by a categorical variable (e.g., region) is the fastest way to spot group-level patterns in a static PCP.
facet_wrap() works with ggparcoord() because it returns a ggplot2 object — leverage the full ggplot2 ecosystem for labels, themes, and layout.
Fix rotated labels with hjust = 1. Rotating alone (angle = 30) causes labels to overlap the plot; hjust = 1 right-aligns them to the tick mark.
parallelPlot enables brushing — clicking an axis variable instantly recolours by value, and selecting a range on any axis filters lines. This makes it far more powerful for interactive discovery.
Add histograms via histoVisibility in parallelPlot to understand marginal distributions without switching to a separate plot.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
A treemap is a space-filling visualisation that encodes hierarchical data as nested rectangles. Two visual channels are used simultaneously:

Rectangle size: quantitative variable (e.g., total units sold)
Rectangle colour: a second quantitative variable (e.g., median unit price)

Treemaps are especially effective for displaying part-to-whole relationships across multiple levels of a hierarchy, such as property transactions grouped by region planning area, and then project name.
This chapter covers three approaches:








Approach
Package
Function(s)




Static treemap (full-featured)
treemap
treemap()


Static treemap (ggplot2-based)
treemapify
geom_treemap() + ggplot2


Interactive treemap
d3treeR
d3tree()

Installing and Launching R Packages

# install.packages("devtools")          # skip if already installed
library(devtools)

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Data Wrangling
The dataset is realis2018.csv, private property transaction records from Singapore’s Urban Redevelopment Authority (URA) REALIS portal for 2018.

Importing the data set

realis2018 <- read_csv("data/realis2018.csv")



Data Wrangling and Manipulation
The raw data is at transaction level (one row per transaction), which is too disaggregated for a treemap. We need to aggregate it to project level, computing:

Total Unit Sold = sum of units per project
Total Area = sum of floor area per project
Median Unit Price ($ psm) = median unit price per project
Median Transacted Price = median transacted price per project

Grouping variables: Project Name, Planning Region, Planning Area, Property Type, Type of Sale.
Two dplyr verbs do this: group_by() and summarise().


Grouped Summaries without the Pipe

realis2018_grouped <- group_by(realis2018, `Project Name`,
                               `Planning Region`, `Planning Area`,
                               `Property Type`, `Type of Sale`)

realis2018_summarised <- summarise(realis2018_grouped,
                          `Total Unit Sold`          = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Total Area`               = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Median Transacted Price`  = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))


Note: na.rm = TRUE is required in all aggregation functions (sum(), median()) because any NA in the input would otherwise propagate to the output, silently producing missing aggregates.

This approach names every intermediate object, which gets unwieldy in longer pipelines.


Grouped Summaries with the Pipe
The pipe operator %>% passes the output of one function directly into the next, eliminating intermediate objects:

realis2018_summarised <- realis2018 %>%
  group_by(`Project Name`, `Planning Region`,
           `Planning Area`, `Property Type`,
           `Type of Sale`) %>%
  summarise(`Total Unit Sold`           = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Total Area`                = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Transacted Price`   = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))


Preferred style: The pipe version is more readable and idiomatic in tidyverse workflows. Both produce identical results.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Designing Treemap with treemap Package
treemap() is the core function of the treemap package, offering 43+ arguments for fine-grained control.

Designing a Static Treemap
First, filter to the property type of interest:

realis2018_selected <- realis2018_summarised %>%
  filter(`Property Type` == "Condominium", `Type of Sale` == "Resale")




Using the Basic Arguments

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")








Three core arguments:








Argument
Purpose
Rule




index
Defines the hierarchy levels
Must have at least two column names; first = highest level


vSize
Maps rectangle size
Column must contain non-negative values only


vColor
Maps rectangle colour
Used together with type to determine colour encoding




Common mistake: Without specifying type, treemap() defaults to type = "index", which colours rectangles by their hierarchy group rather than by the vColor variable’s actual values. This produces a misleading result where the rectangles are coloured categorically by planning area, not by unit price intensity.




Working with vColor and type Arguments
Setting type = "value" corrects the colour encoding so rectangles reflect the numeric values of vColor:

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "value",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")









Rectangles now show different intensities of green reflecting median unit prices.
The legend is binned into equal-interval bins (e.g., 0–5000, 5000–10000, …).




Colours in treemap Package
The type argument controls how the vColor values are mapped to a colour palette. Two key types:








type
Palette assumption
Colour mapping




"value"
Diverging palette (e.g., RdYlBu)
0 → midpoint colour; ±max(abs) → ends. Treats the palette as centred at zero.


"manual"
Any palette
min(values) → left end; max(values) → right end; mean(range) → midpoint. Linear mapping.



Two relevant arguments work alongside type:

palette: name of the ColorBrewer (or other) palette to use.
mapping: controls the exact value-to-colour mapping (auto-set by type).




The "value" Type Treemap

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "value",
        palette      = "RdYlBu",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")









Why no red? The “value” type maps 0 to the middle colour and negative values to the red end of RdYlBu. Since all median unit prices are positive, only the yellow-to-blue half of the palette is used, thus no red appears.


Legend range: Displays only the actual data range (e.g., 5,000–45,000) with pretty rounding, not the full palette range.




The "manual" Type Treemap

# Avoid: diverging palette with all-positive data is confusing
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "RdYlBu",   # problematic for all-positive values
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")









Problem: With type = "manual", the midpoint colour (yellow in RdYlBu) is placed at mean(range(values)), and both red and blue ends appear, creating a confusing impression that some values are negative or below a threshold. Use a sequential palette instead when all values are on one side.


# Correct: sequential palette for all-positive data
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "Blues",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")









Palette selection rule: Use diverging palettes (RdYlBu, RdBu) only when data has a meaningful midpoint (e.g., z-scores, change from baseline). Use sequential palettes (Blues, Greens, YlOrRd) for all-positive or all-negative data.




Treemap Layout
treemap() supports two layout algorithms:








Algorithm
Reference
Behaviour




"squarified"
Bruls et al. (2000)
Optimises aspect ratios (rectangles close to square); ignores sortID


"pivotSize"
Bederson et al. (2002)
Respects sortID ordering while maintaining acceptable aspect ratios. Default.





Working with the algorithm Argument

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "Blues",
        algorithm    = "squarified",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")










Using sortID
sortID only takes effect when algorithm = "pivotSize". It determines the order in which rectangles are placed from top-left to bottom-right:

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type         = "manual",
        palette      = "Blues",
        algorithm    = "pivotSize",
        sortID       = "Median Transacted Price",
        title        = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")









Use sortID to place the most important or highest-value items in the top-left, where the eye naturally lands first.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Designing Treemap using treemapify Package
treemapify integrates treemaps into ggplot2 using geom_treemap() as a layer. This gives access to the full ggplot2 ecosystem, including scales, themes, labels, and more.

Designing a Basic Treemap

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`),
       layout = "scol",
       start  = "bottomleft") +
  geom_treemap() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light blue", high = "blue")








Key aesthetics:



Aesthetic
Maps to




area
Rectangle size (quantitative, non-negative)


fill
Rectangle colour




layout and start control tile arrangement direction.
scale_fill_gradient() gives direct control over the colour ramp, without the need to choose between "value" and "manual" types.




Defining Hierarchy
treemapify uses subgroup and subgroup2 aesthetics (in aes()) to create hierarchical nesting.
Level 1. Group by Planning Region:

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area     = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill     = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`),
       start = "topleft") +
  geom_treemap()








Level 2. Group by Planning Region and Planning Area:

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area      = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill      = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup  = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) +
  geom_treemap()








Adding boundary lines between hierarchy levels:

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area      = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill      = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup  = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup2_border(colour = "gray40", size = 2) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour  = "gray20")








Border geoms for hierarchy levels:



Geom
Draws border around




geom_treemap_subgroup_border()
Level 1 groups (subgroup)


geom_treemap_subgroup2_border()
Level 2 groups (subgroup2)




Tip: Use darker or thicker borders for higher-level groups (Planning Region) and lighter/thinner borders for lower-level groups (Planning Area) to reinforce the visual hierarchy.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Designing Interactive Treemap using d3treeR
d3treeR wraps a static treemap object in a D3.js-powered interactive widget, adding drill-down zoom - clicking a rectangle zooms into that group, revealing its sub-rectangles.

Designing an Interactive Treemap
The workflow is two steps: build the static treemap, then wrap it with d3tree().
Step 1 — Build and save the static treemap:

tm <- treemap(realis2018_summarised,
              index        = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area"),
              vSize        = "Total Unit Sold",
              vColor       = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
              type         = "value",
              title        = "Private Residential Property Sold, 2017",
              title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")









The treemap object tm is saved (not just plotted) by assigning it to a variable.
Note: realis2018_summarised (all property types) is used here, not the filtered realis2018_selected.

Step 2 — Wrap with d3tree() for interactivity:

d3tree(tm, rootname = "Singapore")






rootname: label shown at the top level before any drill-down.
Interaction: Clicking any rectangle zooms into that group; clicking the breadcrumb trail at the top zooms back out.


Key advantage: d3treeR adds drill-down navigation without requiring a different data structure — it reuses the treemap object directly.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Key Takeaways

Always specify type in treemap(). The default type = "index" colours by hierarchy group, not by vColor values.
Match palette type to data. Use diverging palettes (RdYlBu) only when values span a meaningful zero. For all-positive data (like prices), use sequential palettes (Blues, YlOrRd). Using a diverging palette on one-sided data implies a false midpoint.
vSize must be non-negative. Negative sizes are geometrically undefined for rectangle area, need to check the data before plotting.
sortID only works with algorithm = "pivotSize" (the default). If switch to "squarified", sort order is ignored.
treemapify is the ggplot2 way. It’s the best choice when you need to combine treemaps with other ggplot2 features, such as faceting, custom themes, additional annotation layers.
d3treeR converts any treemap object to interactive. The two-step workflow (build tm, then d3tree(tm)) means you can prototype statically and interactivise with one extra line.
Hierarchy requires at least two levels in treemap()’s index. A single-level index produces a flat (non-nested) treemap with no visible grouping structure.
